post.html:
Before you begin

Before you get along with this guide, make sure that you’ve python and any version of PyCharm (community or Professional) installed. If you haven’t install them now.

Creating a new project in PyCharm

  • Open PyCharm, click on Create New Project

  • Next select the directory for your project. Best way is to create the directory on your hard drive first and then just ask PyCharm to create project in that directory.

As you can see, I’ve set the project directory on my desktop inside a folder called PyDemo.

  • Now select python version you installed. In my case, I’ve multiple versions of python installed so it looks like this

In your case, you just pick the one you’ve installed. Now what if your python installation doesn’t show up? Click on the gear button and select your python installation directory from there. PyCharm will index it so you can use the python installation for any project later on.

Then click Add Local and select python executable from the directory where you installed it.

  • When you’re done, click Create

  • Now you should see something like this

  • Congrats! You’ve created your project in PyCharm

Adding your python script

Right click on your project name, then New > Python File. Follow the pictures for better guidance.

You don’t need to add any file extension, PyCharm will add it for you. Just name your file.

Running your script

Since you’re running the script for the very first time, you’ll need to add a run config for that script. Luckily, PyCharm does that automatically.

While your script/ code file is open in the editor (double click to open if you’re on some other file) right click to bring up the context menu and then click Run yourFile. You can also use the keyboard shortcut.

Once you run the file, the config gets added automatically. And from now, you can just click on the play icon on the menubar to run your code.

Note: If you add another python script to your project and want to run it separately you’ll need to add a config for that as well. Just follow the same steps you did for the previous file.

Aaaaaand that’s it!

authornote.html: Shawon Ashraf Hails from Dhaka, Bangladesh. A forever Java guy who’s tinkering with JavaScript and Swift these days. A long time RPG fan who plays Overwatch like frenzy these days. I like to talk anything *nix and OSS.
